--- SOURCE 2: Digital Preservation And Its Challenges - DCIG ---
URL: https://dcig.com/2022/05/digital-preservation-and-its-challenges/

SUMMARY:
Obsolescence. Rapid technological change impacts digital preservation. New technologies appear, older technologies lose commercial viability

FULL CONTENT:
![DCIG logo](https://dcig.com/wp-content/uploads/2021/12/DCIG-logo.png)

# Digital Preservation and its Challenges

Note: *I had the opportunity to work with Fujifilm on a new white paper discussing how Fujifilm’s Object Archive Software helps institutions deal with the challenges of digital preservation. This first of a two-part article is derived from this content and speaks to the challenges of preserving our digital history.*

The concept of digital preservation is analogous to a museum where objects of![Picture of computer button with digital preservation imprinted](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7 "Digital Preservation and its Challenges 1")![Picture of computer button with digital preservation imprinted](https://dcig.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Digital-Preservation-300x214.jpeg "Digital Preservation and its Challenges 2") historical, scientific, or artistic interest are kept. The world preserves these items because of their cultural significance and to steer future decisions through remembering the past.

![Picture of computer button with digital preservation imprinted](data:image/gif;base64,R0lGODlhAQABAIAAAAAAAP///yH5BAEAAAAALAAAAAABAAEAAAIBRAA7 "Digital Preservation and its Challenges 1")
![Picture of computer button with digital preservation imprinted](https://dcig.com/wp-content/uploads/2022/05/Digital-Preservation-300x214.jpeg "Digital Preservation and its Challenges 2")

Historically, important objects like records, maps, books, and art were made where one could see, touch or hold them. In the 1990s, a shift occurred with the rapid rise of computers and the Internet. Today, nearly all vital information or expression of historical, educational, scientific, or cultural significance is created or distributed digitally through computer technology and the Internet. Where meaningful, this digital heritage needs to be preserved regardless of discipline, language, or geography.

## The Challenges of Digital Preservation

With increasingly powerful computers and cloud-based platforms, one might believe that society forgets nothing. However, digital preservation faces threats and risks different from its analog counterparts.

**Corruption**. Corruption to the digital object itself can lead to complete loss. Bit-rot or electromagnetic decay within storage media can happen as digital collections grow large.

**Machine dependencies**. Uniquely, digital materials depend on hardware and software technology for accessibility. If hardware, media, or software stops working, historical files can no longer be accessed.

**Obsolescence**. Rapid technological change impacts digital preservation. New technologies appear, older technologies lose commercial viability and become obsolete.

**Scalability**. Data growth brings challenges for storing accumulating data. Simple strategies for storing photos on a hard drive fail to scale when dealing with extensive, growing, mixed media collections of digital documents, images, audios, and videos.

**Legal issues**. Collecting and storing digital items bring copyright complexities, distribution legalities, and government regulations for storing and transmitting preserved data.

**Ongoing management**. Digital content requires ongoing commitment, policies, budgets, skills, and management. A lack in any of these areas threatens digital preservation.

## The Cloud for Digital Preservation

Certainly, storage serves as a critical aspect of digital preservation and cloud storage presents itself as a reasonable solution. One of the great benefits of the cloud is its simple value proposition: immediate availability of scalable compute and storage infrastructure. However, cloud infrastructure brings challenges for archiving historical documents and media.

**Data ownership**. Organizations risk losing chain-of-custody, audit trail, and access management in cloud services. In some cases, digital rights for data stored or generated within the cloud transfers to the cloud provider through the services agreement.

**Data sovereignty**. The rise of cloud computing has resulted in countries passing regulations that specify how data is stored. Regulations may require data originating within a country’s borders to remain within those borders. It may also mean that data is subject to the jurisdiction in which the cloud infrastructure physically resides.

**Data protection and security**. The accessibility and volume of data stored in the cloud make it an attractive target for criminals. Misconfigurations can expose data to unauthorized users. For data protection, cloud storage does not automatically include data backup. Each cloud vendor handles data differently requiring the customer to know how to deploy back-ups or replication for data protection.

**Cloud cost overruns**. While storing data in the cloud is relatively inexpensive, the cost of frequently accessing that data adds up. Further, it is not always clear how cloud decisions will affect an invoice. It is a common experience for organizations to receive an unexpectedly high cloud services bill at some point.

Certainly, there are good reasons why organizations should leverage the public cloud for digital preservation. The ‘however’ is that for an institution’s primary archival storage, the cloud does not scale cost-effectively and brings challenges for digital preservation.

In part 2, we’ll review why many institutions find on-premises object storage solutions (private cloud) attractive for digital preservation and how [Fujifilm’s Object Archive Software](https://datastorage-na.fujifilm.com/object-archive/) benefits an institution’s digital preservation program.

--- SOURCE 3: Obsolescence - Digital Preservation: A Critical Vocabulary ---
URL: https://digital-preservation-a-critical-vocabulary.pubpub.org/pub/ajzjdqk4

SUMMARY:
by E Cochrane · 2024 — Obsolescence presents many challenges to the digital preservation community. From Software, to hardware, front fonts to Foundations,

FULL CONTENT:
# Obsolescence

**Obsolescence**

Obsolescence is a term that is widely used in the digital preservation literature.Â There are many forms of obsolescence that present themselves in the course of undertaking digital preservation. The following forms of obsolescence will be discussed in this chapter:

File Format Obsolescence

Software Obsolescence

Font Obsolescence

Hardware Obsolescence

Organizational Obsolescence

Each of these differs in its importance and impact on the digital preservation endeavour, but all have their basis in some form of change causing challenges due to something becoming unavailable or out of use.

We see this in the Wikipedia definition of Obsolescence, which is as follows:

**Obsolescence** is the process of becoming antiquated, out of date, old-fashioned, no longer in general use, or no longer useful, or the condition of being in such a state. When used in a biological sense, it means imperfect or rudimentary when compared with the corresponding part of other organisms. The international standard IEC 62402:2019 Obsolescence Management defines obsolescence as the "transition from available to unavailable from the manufacturer in accordance with the original specification".

Â Obsolescence frequently occurs because a replacement has become available that has, in sum, more advantages compared to the disadvantages incurred by maintaining or repairing the original. Obsolete also refers to something that is already disused or discarded, or antiquated. Typically, obsolescence is preceded by a gradual decline in popularity.âÂ Â

Â This definition agreed by the Wikipedia community emphasizes both the process nature of obsolescence and that something can be obsolescing without being obsolete. Wikipediaâs definition is also corroborated by one of the early papers on file format obsolescence:

*âObsolescence describes a state of becoming obsolete, rather than a state of already being obsolete. For our purposes, however, we are interested in identifying file formats affected by both - obviously related - conditions:*

*those that can no longer be rendered (and are therefore obsolete); and*

*those that are likely to become unrenderable within a timeframe demanding action (and are therefore obsolescent).*

*For convenience, we refer to both conditions with the label file format obsolescence, which we believe is in line with common usage in the digital preservation community, even though it may invite some ambiguityâ* *(Pearson and Webb 2008).*

# File Format Obsolescence

File format obsolescence appears to happen on a regular basis. File formats appear to fall out of use due to software packages that implement them becoming less accessible or no longer being available on the market. For example the Microsoft Chart format was supported in Microsoft Excel for a number of versions but is no longer supported in any currently sold version of Microsoft Excel1. Many vendors continue to support old formats in their software when releasing new ones but configure their software to default to using their newest format. The most recent versions of Adobe Acrobat default to creating the most recent versions of the PDF format however they also support the most recent previous versions also. This behaviour of defaulting to newer file formats causes most users to switch to the new format due to nudge behaviour2. After some time, vendors drop support for old formats3 for a variety of reasons, or make them difficult to enable access to in their software. After this process is complete it could be argued that the file formats are now âobsoleteâ as the current version of the software package that created them no longer supports them.

Others argue otherwise, however. David Rosenthal for example, has argued that file formats virtually never obsolesce as there is always a software application that can open them, be it the original application, or an open-source application that can be recompiled for modern Operating Systems (OSs) and hardware. Moreover, the original software can often be âemulatedâ, which involved using a modern computer to simulate an older computer in order to run older software on the simulated computer. Emulation can potentially be used into the indefinite future through use of techniques such as nested emulation (running an emulator in an emulator), or packaging emulators in standard packages that only depend on a single application, so that only that one dependency needs to be recreated for future computers.

But this raises a question â why are we talking about software so much when this section is meant to be about file formats?

A highly important observation regarding all this commentary is that the entire understanding of âfile format obsolescenceâ as a digital preservation issue seems flawed. Why should the community care if a file format is no longer in regular use? File formats, and the documents or software that define them, stay the same forever once created. Any variation creates (by definition) a different file format (standard). We can and do use very old formats today, such as the Comma Separated Values (CSV) file format for storing data and old file formats pose no significant issues for digital preservation. Every old file format continues to provide a structure for storing data in the same way they did when they were first documented. It is also very technically straight forward to reverse file format obsolescence; one must merely start using the format (standard) again. One might even decide to become a retro-file format enthusiast and begin marketing campaigns for old file formats. Developers of new software could implement the old file formats in their modern applications. Of course that is all rather absurd. Why would anyone care to revive an old file format?

The argument here is that file formats and the related idea of âfile format obsolescenceâ are far less important in digital preservation that is emphasised in literature and policy. It doesnât really matter if file formats are not widely used any more. What instead matters is whether we still have access to software that can be used to interact with the files of a particular format and whether we can continue to re-present an âoriginalâ information-experience to the end user using that software.

In other words, a seemingly substantially more significant issue than âfile format obsolescenceâ is the existence of software obsolescence. When software goes out of regular use, digital preservation practitioners are presented with real, meaningful challenges. Â

Â

# Software obsolescence

Â Â âeven in these simplest of cases, sensitivity to the circumstances of use is crucial to determining what is to be preservedâwhat counts as successful preservationâ (Levy 1998)

To adequately discuss software obsolescence, we need to understand what software is. The Wikipedia communityâs definition of software is:

*âSoftware can be defined more narrowly as the instructions executed by the computer hardwareâ* (Wikipedia Contributors 2024)

If we look to the Open Archival Information Systems Standard (ISO 14721), its authors slotted software into their digital âarchivingâ model in the role of âRepresentation Informationâ:

*âThe information that maps a Data Object into more meaningful concepts..â¦.*

*A[n].. example is JPEG software which is used to render a JPEG file; rendering the JPEG file as bits is not very meaningful to humans but the software, which embodies an understanding of the JPEG standard, maps the bits into pixels which can then be rendered as an image for human viewing.â*

![](https://resize-v3.pubpub.org/eyJidWNrZXQiOiJhc3NldHMucHVicHViLm9yZyIsImtleSI6Imd6a2h4ODN2LzIxNzE3OTMxMzIxNTk2LnBuZyIsImVkaXRzIjp7InJlc2l6ZSI6eyJ3aWR0aCI6ODAwLCJmaXQiOiJpbnNpZGUiLCJ3aXRob3V0RW5sYXJnZW1lbnQiOnRydWV9fX0=)

A diagram of a representation Description automatically generated

(CCSDS Secretariat 2012)

This definition would describe an office document, e.g. a â.docâ file, as a âData Objectâ and the software as the âRepresentation Informationâ that ârenders the [.doc] file as bitsâ¦[it] embodies an understanding of the [.doc] standard, maps the bits into pixels which then can be rendered as a [document] for human viewingâ. The definition of software is quite removed from Wikipediaâs and suffers from a common flaw of assuming that all software does (in the digital archiving/preservation context) is ârenderâ âData Objectsâ. Software does a lot more than render objects. When we use a computer we do more than just look at things, we provide instructions to the computer using tapes, swipes, key presses, mouse inputs, button presses and more. And in response to those instructions the computers react to us. In other words, we *interact* with computers and the software on them is itself *interactive*. It takes inputs and responds to them with outputs *interactively.* As an instruction set (per the Wikipedia definition), software combined with hardware provides an interactive experience. This was recognized in the National Archives of Australiaâs âPerformance modelâ:

![](https://resize-v3.pubpub.org/eyJidWNrZXQiOiJhc3NldHMucHVicHViLm9yZyIsImtleSI6InZ0OGk4YzNxLzAxNzE3OTMxMzIxNTk3LnBuZyIsImVkaXRzIjp7InJlc2l6ZSI6eyJ3aWR0aCI6ODAwLCJmaXQiOiJpbnNpZGUiLCJ3aXRob3V0RW5sYXJnZW1lbnQiOnRydWV9fX0=)

A diagram of a process Description automatically generated

(Heslop, Davis and Wilson 2002)

The Performance model situates software as part of the process. The authors state:

â*More specifically, the source of a digital record is a data file. This data file has a defined structure that varies according to different formats: a Microsoft Word document, a Microsoft Excel Spreadsheet, an Adobe Acrobat file and an HTML web page all use different data formats. The process is the specific combination of computer hardware and software and the configuration needed to understand the file format of a source. A Word source requires the correct version of the Word application, using a Windows operating system, which is installed on a suitable Intel computer. The performance is what is rendered to the screen or to any other output device*.â (Heslop, Davis and Wilson 2002)

![](https://resize-v3.pubpub.org/eyJidWNrZXQiOiJhc3NldHMucHVicHViLm9yZyIsImtleSI6IjhxaGxnandlLzIxNzE3OTMxMzIxNTk3LnBuZyIsImVkaXRzIjp7InJlc2l6ZSI6eyJ3aWR0aCI6ODAwLCJmaXQiOiJpbnNpZGUiLCJ3aXRob3V0RW5sYXJnZW1lbnQiOnRydWV9fX0=)

A diagram of software and software Description automatically generated

What they fail to highlight is not just the interactivity of performances or âinformation experiencesâ, but that the software included in this model is itself also made up of data files. Software is (just) a set of instructions, the âdata filesâ examples cited in the performance model are also just sets of instructions. In other words, the âData Filesâ or âData Objectsâ of the OAIS are themselves software. This is important as we have built our digital preservation systems around a distinction between these so-called âdata filesâ and the other files that make up the software that we use for interacting with them to produce information experiences. We preference the âdata filesâ perhaps because they are rarer than the software files, more likely to be unique. However virtually no files these days are truly unique (due to backups and redundancies in storage) and software files also have the potential to be almost unique as they go out of use.

Most importantly though, software files provide more of the content thatâs used to make up many of our information experiences than the âdata filesâ do. Outside of large-file types such as video files, the files that make up the software component of an information experience are often cumulatively much bigger in size than the âdata filesâ are. A simple example is presented by this very document. As I write it the document is a âdocxâ Office Open XML file and only a few hundred kilobytes in size. Whereas the âWINWORD.EXEâ software file that I opened to write this document with is 1600KB alone, and the other files that make up the âOffice16â folder it is in are over 1.73GB in size. Even that still ignores the files that make up the software add-ins, Operating System, drivers, and BIOS files. What Iâm aiming to emphasise here is the absurdity of ignoring the software files as being a key component of the information experiences we are (presumably) trying to preserve in digital preservation. We have never established that the âdata filesâ are more important to the experience than the software files4. If anything, we have established that they are both essential. This in turn is important as obsolescence refers to things going out of use. If we are to do our jobs properly, and all digital objects include software, and weâre trying to preserve digital objects, then perhaps our goal should be to ensure that software never goes out of use?

If we return to file formats, file formats adhere to some sort of standard. The standard may be formally documented and approved by some sort of governing body, as the original Office Open XML standards were5, or will be de-facto and defined by how a piece of software was programmed by its developer to encode the files it creates. In the former case software developers interpret file format standards to produce instructions for computers. Those instructions enable them to work with both the instructions in the other software files, in the computersâ operating system and bios software files, and in those Data Files/Objects, along with instructions gained from hardware inputs given to the computer that is executing the instructions. In other words, you have a myriad of instructions that the computer follows:

The instructions in the computersâ bios software files (normally hard-coded into a memory chip

The instructions in the computersâ Operating System software files

The instructions in the applicationâs files, modern applications often are made up of many hundreds or thousands of files full of instructions to be executed for every conceivable input set

The instructions included in the software files provided by add-ons and other applications that the âprimaryâ application relies on

The instructions provided via hardware inputs by a user or another system

To assume a software developer will ever be able successfully implement a âfile format standardâ in such a way as to ensure that the files their software creates always behave the same on any other system that uses them as a member of their instruction sets, seems like a significantly challenging expectation. Itâs a challenging expectation for several reasons:

The software developer has to be able to understand the file format standard.

The software developer has to ensure their software meets their understanding of the standard when encoding instructions into files.

The software developer has to make a variety of assumptions about the other instructions any computer will be following when it interprets those in the files their software is going to create.

The following is an excerpt from one of the sections in the .doc word binary file format standard regarding how to store information about paragraphs in a document:

***rgfc (variable):*** *An array of 4-byte unsigned integers. Each element of this array specifies an offset in the* *[WordDocument Stream](file:///C:\Users\ecoch\Downloads\%5bMS-DOC%5d-240521.docx#Section_d7fae142670d4cd5869a708366984a71)* *where a paragraph of text begins, or where an* ***[end of row mark](file:///C:\Users\ecoch\Downloads\%5bMS-DOC%5d-240521.docx#gt_9fe5b7d0-b009-4e3b-9368-10a2a3ab8da9)*** *exists. This array MUST be sorted in ascending order and MUST NOT contain duplicates. Each paragraph begins immediately after the end of the previous paragraph. The count of elements that this array contains is* ***cpara*** *incremented by 1. The last element does not specify the beginning of a paragraph; instead it specifies the end of the last paragraph.*

***rgbx (variable):*** *An array of* ***[BxPap](file:///C:\Users\ecoch\Downloads\%5bMS-DOC%5d-240521.docx#Section_86df4678ff4d4877b61a6c621906973f)****, followed by* ***PapxInFkp*** *structures. The elements of this array, which has* ***cpara*** *elements and parallels* ***rgfc****, each specify the offset of one of the* ***PapxInFkp*** *structures in this* ***PapxFkp*** *structure.*

*Each* ***PapxInFkp*** *specifies the paragraph properties for the paragraph at the corresponding offset in* ***rgfc*** *or the table properties for the table row whose end of row mark is located at the corresponding offset in* ***rgfc****.*

***cpara (1 byte):*** *An unsigned integer that specifies the total number of paragraphs, table rows, or table cells for which this* ***PapxFkp*** *structure specifies formatting. This field occupies the last byte of the* ***PapxFkp*** *structure The value of this field MUST be at least 0x01, and MUST NOT exceed 0x1D because that would cause* ***rgfc*** *and* ***rgb*** *to expand and PapxFkp to exceed 512 bytes.* (Microsoft Corporation 2022)

Here is another, slightly more digestible example from the Open Document Text standard:

*The <text:p> element represents a paragraph, which is the basic unit of text in an OpenDocument file* (Cave, et al. 2021)*.*

Neither of these examples make the job of implementing the ability to write files that adhere to those standards particularly easy for the developer. Even in the simple (ODT) case, instead of complexity leading to a diversity in understanding, the simplicity adds ambiguity and flexibility in understanding and implementation. In practice these situations may well explain why different software interacts with the same files in different ways. This is something we saw in (Cochrane 2012) and every user eventually experiences when they change software applications or entire computers or switch to a new operating system (such as when changing from a Microsoft Windows-based computer to an Apple computer running MacOS). Data files interacted with using one set of software invariably behave differently than those interacted with using another set of software.

In (Cochrane 2012) we also saw that changes that can be introduced when interacting with content in different software than the original are often somewhat obscure but consequential. They are also often different for any specific object, making them hard to predict and possibly rarely is any change noticed enough to produce enough complaints for the software developer to remedy it. Most significantly, it would seem that users rarely examine the detail of their documents when moving between systems. Most often they are likely happy to be able to generally access the object and can rectify any changes themselves as they know/create the objects. If there are significant issues they can reach out to the software developer to have them âfixedâ. However in the use-cases that matter to archivists and digital preservation practitioners, e.g. future researchers, or future lawyers in future court cases attempting to access preserved objects and use them as evidence, it is usually too late to reach out to the developer and also difficult for the user (the researcher or lawyer) to identify what has changed v.s. what was original to the object (e.g. original human âmistakesâ/edits). Because these use cases relate to software that is likely no longer profitable (or even on the market), software developers, unfortunately, donât currently prioritize the future researcher/lawyer use-cases. There is some hope arising however. With the rise of âdeep-fakesâ the public is increasingly interested in being able to verify the integrity of digital objects and specifically, the content they are presenting to users. So there is hope to be found in the possibility that this may lead to new methods for ensuring that digital objectsâ content can be verified both now and in the future.

File format standards being difficult to implement in software further emphasizes that it is not the file formats or their standards that are going obsolete. The standards can be implemented with just as much effort (whether that be a lot or a little) today or in the future as they could when they were first written. It is the software that is at risk of obsolescence and which needs active effort to ensure that it never actually becomes obsolete.

There are endeavours underway to ensure that software never goes obsolete. That it is always âin useâ or at least, always accessible when needed. The Emulation as a Service Infrastructure (EaaSI) program of work6, its network of organizations preserving and using old software, and its offshoot network âAus-EaaSIâ in Australia7 are two examples of these. Such efforts are essential to all of digital preservation if we are to success in combating software obsolescence. As established above, all files are software and since digital preservation is file-focused all digital preservation is software preservation. So combating software obsolescence seems to be the most important goal of digital preservation.

## Font Obsolescence

Font obsolescence, a subset or variant of software obsolescence, happens when a font is no longer in wide use and is difficult or impossible to access. This can cause significant issues with interpretability of documents as a font can effectively encode the information in such a way that it is as difficult to decipher as a complex code.Â

âEven with access to the full documentation for a legacy proprietary format, replicating the behavior of the original environment used to render that document can be extremely difficultâor impossibleâfor tasks as seemingly basic as font selection and use.â (Brown and Woods 2009)

In their foundational paper on the topic âBorn Broken: Fonts and Information Loss in Legacy Digital Documentsâ (Brown and Woods 2009), Brown and Woods analysed 230,000 Word documents to identify font usage patterns and potential issues. They found that fonts often go out of use and are often rarely used (in the case of obscure fonts) and when out of use can cause significant issues for the readability of documents that rely on them if the fonts have not been embedded in the document files themselves. They emphasize that without efforts to address font obsolescence issues up to 8% of documents on average will likely not have their fonts available when researchers attempt to access them in the future. Much like spies encoding text with ciphers that substitute representations of words for other words with different meanings, fonts encode meanings of words with pictorial representations of them. Without the correct fonts to decode the documents the information in them can be locked away and unreadable, just like the secret agentâs enciphered messages.

# Hardware Obsolescence

Hardware obsolescence is causing some of the more challenging issues for digital preservation practitioners. Hardware obsolescence refers to the use of, availability of, and/or access to computer hardware, including peripherals such as mice, keyboards, game controllers, and mobile phones touch screens, diminishing or going away entirely over time. There are two main areas where this is causing issues, when attempting to present the original experience of a digital object to users, including the experience of the hardware, and when validating emulated versions of computers.

When digital preservation practitioners want to be able to present the original experience of a digital object to users it may be necessary to provide access to hardware as part of that experience. This is particularly relevant when preserving access to video games. Video games, in particular arcade and console games, often use and/or used unique hardware for interaction. Video game consoles have communities that are obsessive about their particular controllers that are provided with the consoles and used to play games on them. Online communities discuss details of the controllers, such as the force needed to press a joystick with excruciating detail. They do this because they care, because differences in the force can lead to a different experience of the game.

Unfortunately, computer hardware, particularly peripherals, is often made of materials that donât last over time. From capacitors that degrade, leak, and destroy other components, to plastics that yellow, degrade and wear out, to springs, rubber belts and other small but vital parts, even the best computer hardware has components that simply do not last. This is a huge challenge for digital preservation that fortunately does have some potential resolutions.

## Validating Emulated Computers

In addition to the challenges of presenting original hardware to users as part of providing them unchanged digital object experiences digital preservation practitioners also face hardware obsolescence issues when trying to validate emulated computers. Many digital preservation practitioners want to ensure software can be kept accessible indefinitely (for reasons outlined above). To do this, as hardware goes obsolete practitioners have turned to using emulators8 to ensure software is still able to be interacted with via modern computer hardware. However there is still a need, at the point when an emulator for a particular computer is created, to test and validate that the emulated computer behaves with in the same range of boundary conditions as the original did. While there are some software-based ways to test hardware compatibility (for example early versions of Microsoft Flight Simulator were used to test âIBM PC compatibility[1]) arguably the best test is to compare the emulated computer to original hardware. For this reason some archives (particularly those focused on software preservation) keep original hardware running as long as possible in case their researchers wish to compare an emulated computer to an original9.

## Preserving hardware

To address hardware obsolescence, many archives are attempting to preserve computer hardware. The Living Computer Museum was a great example of such an organization that tried to preserve old computers as living, usable devices. While they have closed, there are many others that are still following this legacy from the Strong Museum of Play in Rochester, New York, to the Computer Spiele Museum in Berlin.

Preserving original hardware that is prone to decay can require novel approaches. The Living Computer Museum went so far as to commission factories to produce new runs of old computer components in order to fix single machines they were trying to revive. Many âretroâ video game enthusiasts will create working versions of old hardware by combining new parts with old or combining multiple broken old devices to create one working one. Many hardware archivists have taken to using 3D printing to create parts to keep old hardware functional (Keene 2024). Finally, some preservation practitioners are simulating old hardware in virtual reality (EmuVR 2024) to provide an experience that is as close to using the original hardware as possible

# Organizational Obsolescence

Our final topic in this journey of long-term change is *Organizational* Obsolescence. Organizational obsolescence refers to the failure of an organization over time. In digital preservation this is a core concern that is baked into community standards. ISO 16363 Audit and Certification of Trustworthy Digital Repositories, one of the few international digital preservation standards places significant emphasis on the health of the âRepositoryâ (Archive):

*âThe repository shall have a mission statement that reflects a commitment to the preservation of, long term retention of, management of, and access to digital information.â* (CCSDS Secretariat 2011, 17)

*âThe repository shall have an appropriate succession plan, contingency plans, and/or escrow arrangements in place in case the repository ceases to operate or the governing or funding institution substantially changes its scope.â* (CCSDS Secretariat 2011, 18)

Ensuring the long-term viability of the organizations that undertake digital preservation is a concern that should be and often is a responsibility of all of society (national Archives and Libraries with a long-term preservation mandate are examples of this). Given the pace of change of digital technology, and the increasing growth of digital content and collecting goals, organizations undertaking digital preservation have a huge challenge ahead of themselves just to stay economically viable. New methods, tools, and technology are constantly needed for digital preservation and while storage costs have been going down over time, the rate at which theyâve been decreasing is beginning to slow as Kryderâs law is slowing ( Santamaria 2024). In other words, the costs to undertake digital preservation appear to be on a consistent growth curve into the indefinite future. To ensure long term viability of organizations undertaking digital preservation, to address organizational obsolescence, the community will need to find some novel solutions in coming years.

# The Obsolescence of Obsolescence

Obsolescence presents many challenges to the digital preservation community. From Software, to hardware, front fonts to Foundations, obsolescence might make digital preservation untenable. But digital preservation is a continual activity and obsolescence is one also. With commitment and funding digital preservation can counter the causes of obsolescence. With dedication digital preservation might even make obsolescence obsolete.

# Bibliography

Santamaria, Steven. 2024. *Why the Slowdown of Kryderâs Law Spells Urgency for Sustainable Archival Storage.* 9th April. Accessed June 9th,

# Problems and Challenges in the Preservation of Digital Contents: Problems and Challenges in the Preservation of Digital Contents: 

# An Analytical Study An Analytical Study 

Usman Ali Shah Khushal Khan Khattak University Karak, Khyber Paktunkhwa, Pakistan , uas833@gmail.com 

Mohammad Hussain Khushal Khan Khattak University Karak, Khyber Paktunkhwa, Pakistan , mhustb@gmail.com 

Ms Saddiqa Khushal Khan Khattak University Karak, Khyber Paktunkhwa, Pakistan , saddiqaktk92@gmail.com 

Muhammad Shehr Yar Khushal Khan Khattak University Karak, Khyber Paktunkhwa, Pakistan , mshehryar020@gmail.com 

Follow this and additional works at:  https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/libphilprac 

Part of the  Library and Information Science Commons 

Shah, Usman Ali; Hussain, Mohammad; Saddiqa, Ms; and Yar, Muhammad Shehr, "Problems and 

Challenges in the Preservation of Digital Contents: An Analytical Study" (2021). 

Library Philosophy and 

Practice (e-journal) . 5628. 

https://digitalcommons.unl.edu/libphilprac/5628 1

Problems and Challenges in the Preservation of Digital Contents: An Analytical Study 

Usman Ali Shah 

DLIS, Khushal Khan Khattak University Karak 

Mohammad Husain 

DLIS, Khushal Khan Khattak University Karak 

Saddiqa 

DLIS, Khushal Khan Khattak University Karak 

Muhammad Shehr Yar 

DLIS, Khushal Khan Khattak University Karak 

Abstract 

This paper probed the issues and changelogs related to the preservation of digital 

contents. A literature -based approach was adopted to identify the major problems related to the 

preservation of these unique contents. After studying the literature, it was identified that basic 

principles that should be kept in mind while preserving digital contents are longevity, selection, 

quality, integrity, and accessibility. It was found that the most critical problems and challenges 

faced during the digital preservatio n including nature of the contents keeping the data, 

maintaining trust in the data, coping with the data deluge, technological obsolescence, media 

fragility, lack of expert and copyright & intellectual property right issues, etc. It was concluded 

that the libraries, archives, and information centers should be equipped with ICT facilities, and 

the staff has the necessary skills to handle the management of digital resources and their 

preservation. Moreover, sufficient funds should be allocated for the procure ment and 

preservation of digital objects. 

Keywords: Digital Contents, Digital Preservation, Problems of Digital Preservation, Principals 

of Digital Preservation 

Introduction 

Developments in science and technology bring on changes in every sector. We used to 

communicate by writing letters in the past. However, technology has transformed that medium 

into the telephone, mobile phone, and, more recently, the internet, which allows us to make video 

calls. Previously, all library activities (acquisition, classification, cataloging, management, 

storing, retrieving, and disseminating, among others) were performed manually by librarians, but 

now information and communication technologie s are used. As computing and library 

technology enhance library services, printers and scanners are utilized to produce copies of 2

digital documents. We face some problems and challenges in preserving printed material, such as 

preservation of knowledge and skills, space constraints, and so on. While there are also problems 

and issues when it comes to preserving digital content, such as hardware, software, expertise, and 

so on. 

Information and Communication Technologies (ICT) is a diverse set of technological 

tools used to communicate, create, manage, store, and disseminate information (Blurton, 1999). 

The use of computers and communication technology facilities the libraries and LIS 

professionals. ICT devices and applications are used in librarianship for ac quiring, organizing, 

storing, retrieving and disseminating of information (Thanuskodi, 201 1).

In 1972 when the internet was developed, e -mail was used as a communication tool; three 

years later, a listserv was also developed in 1975, which was an application used for distributing 

messages among the subscribers. The World Wide Web (WWW) emerged in 1991, which is a 

milestone in the computer and internet industry, and thus the world becomes a global village 

(Preece, Maloney -Krichmar & Abras, 2003). With the emergence of WWW, several internet -

based applications and tools emerged and developed, including search engines, websites, 

databases, social networking sites, etc. Through websites and such like other media, people used 

to share information in the form of text, audio, video, photographs etc. 

Concept of Preservation and Digital Preservation 

Preservation is defined as “the branch of library and information science concerned with 

maintaining or restoring access to artifacts, documents and records through the study, diagnosis, 

treatment and prevention of decay and damage. It should be distinguished from conservation 

which refers to the treatment and repair of individual items to slo w decay or restore them to a 

usable state. Conservation is occasionally used interchangeably with preservation, particularly 

outside the professional literature ” (Reitz, 2004) .

Even though preservation as a formal service in libraries and archives dates fr om the 

twentieth century, it was also practiced in many ancient libraries. Various methods have been 

used in many ancient societies to protect books and manuscripts from insects, fire, and decay. 

Digital preservation, as defined by Tiwari (2015), refers to the maintenance of digital 

content. Digital preservation is a collection of processes, activities, and digital information 

management practices that are implemented over time to ensure long -term accessibility of digital 3

information . The term "digital pres ervation" refers to a set of policies, strategies, and actions that 

ensure long -term access . to digital content (Dappert & Farquhar, 2009). 

The Library of Congress defines digital preservation as “the active management of 

digital content over time to ens ure ongoing access” ((http://www.digitalpreservation.gov/about/). 

Digital preservation is a set of coordinated activities that ensures that digital materials can be 

accessed for as long as they are required (Deegan & Tanner, 2006). 

Emergence of Digital Content 

The late twentieth century saw a tremendous expansion of knowledge. This expansion of 

knowledge has resulted in the publication and distribution of information in all disciplines. This 

was also the era of the digital revolution in the world. The emergence of computers has resulted 

in the discovery, retrieval and creation o f new information in all fields. Early digital work 

allowed for quantitative analysis of political, social, and economic processes by digitizing and 

sorting numbers from computers. In the 1980s, electronic text, hypertext, and the start of e -mail 

arrived; in this period, computers were used for digitizing and linking documents. The internet 

was developed in the 1990s when computers began digitizing images and manipulating visual 

data. The products that are available in digital format are referred to as "dig ital content." It refers 

to music, data, and images that are available for download or distribution on digital media. These 

materials are saved in a specific format on digital or analogue storage (Mullan, 2011). 

Be -digital and born -digital content are the two forms of digital content. Be digital content 

is information that has been converted from analog to digital via reproduction methods such as 

rekeying or scanning. The term "born digital" refers to content created and developed in digital 

form using some technology. Libraries obtain digital resources through various methods, 

including purchasing digital content directly from publishers and vendors. Furthermore, librarie s

and organizations all over the world are working on projects to convert their analog collections 

to digital formats to improve access. 

Why is Digital Preservation Important for Organizations and Libraries? 

Traditional libraries are rapidly evolving into digital libraries, if not entirely, then at least in 

part. Digital preservation is the process of storing data in digital form in order to ensure the 

information's usability, durability, and intellectual integrity . Information technology is employed 

in th e production, distribution, and storage of data (Najar & Wani, 2019). 4

The main goal of digital preservation is to keep digital content safe and accessible for a long 

time ; other objectives are.  

> •

To protect and allow access to the original format of information  

> •

To ensure the authenticity of preserved digital materials  

> •

Preventing physical media damage and deterioration through environmental control  

> •

To undo damages, if possible  

> •

To avoid data loss  

> •

If and when digital materials need to be changed to preserve their intellectual content  

> •

To implement security measures that protect digital data 

Principles of Digital Preservation 

The basic principles employed for preserving digital material are like those principles that 

apply to the preservation of analog/traditional ones. Conway (1996) defined five principles for 

preservation, inclu ding “longevity, choice or selection, quality, integrity, and accessibility that is 

being practiced for the preservation of analog media and can be extended to digital 

preservation ”.

Longevity 

Longevity means long life or long -lasting. The longevity of digital contents is determined 

by the life of the hardware and software used for storage and retrieval of digital data . Digital data 

do not exist forever due to the fragility of digital works . To ensure continuous access and 

availability of digital information, migration of digital contents should be done on a regular 

basis. 

Selection 

Selection plays a significant role in the success of the preservation plan. Whether it is 

selecting material fo r preservation or selecting tools and technology, or selecting media and 

formats, selecting digital content for preservation should reflect the broader institutional mission. 

Quality 

The quality of digital material is determined by the accessibility and us efulness of digital 

content, which is primarily governed by the limitations of capture and display technology. There 

are limitations to the printing and display technology used for the preservation. As a result, such 

devices should be used for storage in o rder to ensure the quality of the data stored when it is later 

used (Conway, 1996) .5

Integrity 

The physical and intellectual integrity of digital content are both important considerations 

in digital preservation. Digital preservation measures the physical i ntegrity of digital resources 

through loss of information when a source is created and compressed mathematically to store or 

transmit via network technology (Lynch, 1994). The information management can maintain 

control over the integrity of digital resources by authenticating access processes and 

documenting successive modifications to a digital file. 

Access 

Information technology equipment provides a preservation solution for the documents of 

libraries and other important organizations, as well as institutions with escalated access to them 

across multiple data networks. When it comes to storing valuable digital content, the most 

important factor to consider is granting access to that content. The purchase of common 

hardware and software will ensure that digital content can be accessed indefinitely. 

Digital Preservation Strategies 

Velmurugan (2013) mentioned three types of strategies for the preservatio n of digital 

objects . These are long -term strategies: maintaining continuous access to digital materials or 

ensuring that the information contained in them is accessible indefinitely. Medium -term strategy: 

gives access to digital materials for a set period of time, but not indefinitely.Short -term strategy: 

Access to digital materials is prov ided for a set period of time while use is anticipated, but it does 

not extend beyond the foreseeable future until it becomes inaccessible due to technological 

changes. 

Issues and Challenges in Digital Preservation 

As digital technology offers many advantages over the printed one . Information 

technology, as well as other related technologies such as the internet and web, are always 

changing.It is difficult for libraries and information resource centers to keep up with the rapidly 

changing technology involved in digital preservation. Early on, a thorough examination of the 

risk factors associated with digital preservation should be identified to assess their potential 

impact. Different scholars identified various issues and challenges regardi ng the preservation of 

digital content. 6

The issues and challenges regarding the preservation of digital content were mentioned 

by researchers, including Arora (2006), Rahman and Mezbah -ul -Islam (2012), and Velmurugan 

(2013) . These issues and problems are discussed as under .

Nature of Digital Contents 

Chen (2001) stated that the most serious issue with digital preservation is the content 

itself. Digital information exists in various types and formats . The majority of objects that are 

exact replicas of their print documents (such as books, reports, correspondence, and so on, can be 

converted into e -documents and preserved easily. However, materials such as interactive web 

pages, geographic information systems, and so on that cannot be replicated in tradi tional hard 

copy may pose challenges for libraries and information centers in terms of preserving these 

objects. 

Fragility of the Media 

Fragility means the quality of something that is quickly deteriorated or broken . Digital 

content storage media are highl y unstable and fragile because they can deteriorate and fail due to 

heat, humidity, airborne contaminants, or faulty reading and writing on devices (Hedstrom &

Montgomery, 1998). The most common issue with digital preservation is media failure or 

deteriora tion, as well as rapid changes in computer hardware and software that make older 

systems obsolete. 

Machine Dependency 

Digital contents are machine -dependent and necessitate specialized hardware and 

software for data storage and retrieval. However, because the computer (hardware and software) 

and storage technologies are constantly evolving, the storage equipment must be changed and 

upgraded accordingly. Otherwise, it could pose the greatest technological threat to the continued 

availability of digital conte nt. 

Maintain Digital Objects 

When a document is converted into digital form, then protecting that digital content from 

altering and deterioration becomes the focus point. In some cases, digital objects had a short life 

span as compared to hard copy (printe d copy). It can be a problem for the concerned to preserve 

objects of such file formats. 7

Formats and Styles 

Information that was once limited to the traditional types, including monographs, 

cartographic materials, charts, graphic contents, etc., but now there are variety of digital contents 

available in various formats . Hypertext, multimedia, dynamic pages, geographic information 

systems, and interactive video are just a few examples of new formats that have been emerged. 

The encoding, compression, an d storage of these file formats or styles present numerous 

challenges to libraries and information centers. 

Lack of Experts 

The digital preservation process requires trained and technologically competent staff to 

manage the resources properly. However, get ting such experts often becomes difficult. 

Improper Budget 

As digital preservation requires the latest and sophisticated technology and experts who 

require additional expenditure for the concerned organization; but if the organization did not 

provide a sufficient budget, the whole digital preservation project would be hampered. 

Copyright and Intellectual Property Rights (IPR) 

The impact of intellectual property rights (IPR) on digital preservation is significant. The 

intellectual property rights (IPR) issues for digital content are more complex than those for 

printed material. IPR problems in the digital world apply not only to digital content but also to all 

related applications. A digital library which offers its resources on the internet is where you can 

preserve and loan what others have produced, but through the stroke of your digital presence you 

can create new copies of their original digital content. 

Some problems regarding the preservation of digital content were also explo red by the 

Digital Preservation Coalition (2015), these are described in the following section .

Keeping the Data (To store Data) 

Data storage on some storage media may be a problem since some media degrade over 

time. A systematic preservation process is a must for ensuring long -term digital preservation. 

Storage media must be observed, monitored, and refreshed on a regular basis. The introduction of 

redundancy through file replication or backup, the introduction of diversity in dependent 

technologies, and the prevention of catastrophic disasters at a single geographical location —the 

locations in which digital materials are stored, as well as the responsibility for maintaining them, 

should be meticulously recorded and assigned. 8

Keeping the Meaning of the Data 

Information reconstruction usually requires the use of computer software that can render, 

manipulate, and interact with the data's encoding or format. The formats of files may change 

over time and the interacting software applications may become obsolete. It may be possible to 

render an old file format by emulating some obsolete software, but whether running th e software 

is legal, how accurate the rendering will be, and how much will this cost are all questions that 

need to be answered. 

Maintaining Trust in the Data 

Digital materials may remain fluid in the long term, edited or altered easily, damaged by 

media failure, or inaccurately or inconsistently decoded into human -readable information 

through rendering software. For an end user to rely on the digital preservati on work, carefully 

considering the complete life cycle of digital materials and who or what has interacted with them 

over time. The use of the data integrity method and the preservation of assessment trails can 

ensure that the digital object has not change d since it was stored in the archive. Finally, the 

overall fidelity of the preservation organization may influence a user's perception of its 

authenticity. 

Acting in a Timely Manner 

It means that digital objects should be preserved in such a way that users can get the most 

out of them in the least amount of time. Prioritizing and carrying out digital preservation 

activities on time may be necessary to avoid losses and make the best use of resources. 

Coping with the Data Deluge 

According to David Rosenthal's research report, "the rate of data generation is increasing 

by about 60 per cent per year; data storage is increasing by about 25 per cent per year, and data 

center budgets are increasing by about 2 per cent per year" (Baker et al., 2006) .This r eport shows 

that the rate of data creation is much greater than the volume of storage. The effectively 

functioning of simple preservation processes will not necessarily scale easily to work with a data 

deluge or perhaps huge individual files. The administr ative and technical challenges of acquiring, 

managing, and providing access to digital materials on such a large scale should not be 

overlooked. It is necessary to note that selection, acquisition, accessioning, assessment, and 

disposal are essential eleme nts in any digital management practice. 9

Conclusion 

In the age of Science and Technology, the preservation of documents is now facilitated 

by Information technology known as digital preservation. This paper is based on previously 

published literature. Afte r studying the literature, it was concluded that basic principles must 

have to keep in mind in preserving digital contents are longevity, selection, quality, integrity, and 

accessibility. It was seen that the most critical problems/challenges faced during the process of 

digital preservation such as; nature of the contents (i -e; “made digital” or “born digital”); keeping 

the data; maintaining trust in the data; coping with the data deluge; technological obsolescence; 

media fragility; lack of expert and copyr ight and Intellectual Property Rights and information 

centers with the necessary skills to handle the management of digital resources and its 

preservation. 10

--- SOURCE 5: Digital Preservation Challenges and Solutions ---
URL: https://siarchives.si.edu/what-we-do/digital-curation/digital-preservation-challenges-and-solutions

SUMMARY:
Formats that are optimal for long-term preservation and access tend to be open, well established, and not dependent on only one software application, hardware, or operating system. If the Archives receives a digital file that is not already in its accepted preservation format, it will determine if the file is at immediate risk for obsolescence, and if so, will migrate it into the preservation format if possible. In some cases, a preservation format might also serve as the access format for a digital file. There are multiple software applications that can access PDF files. Either special viewer/conversion software or newer Microsoft Word software is used to access a copy of the file and migrate it to its preservation format of PDF/A or PDF.

FULL CONTENT:
![Smithsonian Institution Archives](/sites/all/themes/siaomega/images/logo.svg)

## Search Google Appliance

# Digital Preservation Challenges and Solutions

There are some of the common challenges that archives and other organizations face regarding digital preservation.

## Proprietary and Obsolete Formats

Formats that are optimal for long-term preservation and access tend to be open, well established, and not dependent on only one software application, hardware, or operating system. If the Archives receives a digital file that is not already in its accepted preservation format, it will determine if the file is at immediate risk for obsolescence, and if so, will migrate it into the [preservation format if possible](https://siarchives.si.edu/sites/default/files/pdfs/SIA_format_conversion_table_2016.pdf).

## Accessibility of Files

In some cases, a preservation format might also serve as the access format for a digital file. A collection might include a set of word-processing files that were created in [XyWrite](https://en.wikipedia.org/wiki/XyWrite) decades ago. The XyWrite application was mostly used in DOS and Windows environments in the late 1980s and early 1990s.  These files have been migrated to [PDF/A](https://www.pdfa.org/), which should be accessible on current and older computers with the proper software. There are multiple software applications that can access PDF files.

On the other hand, a research request might include a set of audio files. The Archives preservation format for audio is Broadcast WAV, which can be uncompressed and contains embedded metadata. These uncompressed files can be large and difficult to quickly deliver to a researcher. Instead the access files would be delivered as MP3 files because they are smaller and the audio quality difference is not typically apparent to most listeners.

## Using Storage and Backups Effectively

While storage costs continue to decline, the growth of digital records is exponentially increasing. Cheaper storage can sometimes lead to the false notion that everything can be saved just because there is space for it. Digital records need to be managed effectively in order to be useful. This means making thoughtful decisions about retaining file versions (draft vs. final), consulting [records schedules](https://siarchives.si.edu/what-we-do/forums/records-management-smithsonian-staff/smithsonian-records-disposition-schedule), using informative and consistent file names, and adding metadata.

Another cost of operating online storage is the energy required to power machines, especially when it is available 24/7. Offline storage still requires management so that digital records can be retrieved in a timely manner when needed. Archives and libraries also need to regularly forecast storage needs as digital collections grow.

The Archives’ storage needs are fulfilled by network servers behind a firewall at a Smithsonian data center that are backed up regularly to multiple tapes, and one copy is kept offsite as a best practice. As the machines age, the Smithsonian plans for the eventual replacement to continue keeping digital collections free from corruption and remaining accessible.

## Planning Ahead for Software, Hardware, and Operating Systems Obsolescence

Software developers update and improve their applications. Hardware slows down as it ages and manufacturers build faster machines. Operating systems also evolve and adapt to changes in computing environments. The Archives monitors these developments and plans for how its digital collections might be affected. If an accession arrives that contains a 3.5” floppy diskette with old Microsoft Word 97 documents, staff needs to have and maintain the proper equipment and procedures to access both the obsolete media and files. In this case, the Archives is able to use a USB-connected 3.5” drive to a PC to transfer those files off the media. Either special viewer/conversion software or newer Microsoft Word software is used to access a copy of the file and migrate it to its preservation format of PDF/A or PDF.

enriched_points.expanded_content: Rapid advancements in technology lead to obsolescence, making older formats and systems unusable, as new software and hardware replace them, threatening access to digital archives.
Managing multiple versions of digital files complicates preservation, as different iterations may contain unique data, requiring thorough tracking to maintain integrity and avoid confusion.
Continuous monitoring, updates, and migrations are essential to ensure that digital assets remain accessible and functional, as digital formats and storage media evolve over time.
